name: Yaman
pakad: N R G M(t) D N S'
description: One of the most foundational and widely taught ragas of Hindustani music. Its use of tivra Ma gives it an elevated, expansive quality. Often the first raga a student learns in Kalyan thaat.

name: Bhairav
pakad: S r G M P d N S'
description: A morning raga associated with the deity Shiva. The komal Re and komal Dha create a uniquely austere and devotional atmosphere. Widely used in bhajans and dhrupad compositions.

name: Bhairavi
pakad: S r g M P d n S'
description: Known as the queen of ragas and traditionally the last raga performed in a concert. Uses all four komal swaras, giving it a deeply emotional and devotional character. Extremely versatile — heard in classical, semi-classical, and film music alike.

name: Todi
pakad: r g M(t) P d N S'
description: Considered one of the most complex and emotionally rich ragas. The combination of komal Re, komal Ga, and tivra Ma creates an intensely yearning quality. A raga for experienced performers — demands meticulous intonation.

name: Marwa
pakad: S r G M(t) D N S'
description: A raga of twilight and transition. The absence of Pa (Pancham) and the prominent komal Re lend it an unresolved, searching quality. Often described as evoking the mood just before sunset.

name: Purvi
pakad: G M(t) P d N S' N d P
description: A grave and majestic raga performed at dusk. Shares its scale with Todi but differs in its characteristic phrases and emotional weight. The juxtaposition of tivra Ma and komal Dha creates a distinctive austere color.

name: Kafi
pakad: S R g M P D n S'
description: Associated with the spring season and the Holi festival. The komal Ga and komal Ni give it a folk-like quality. Closely related to the Dorian mode in Western music. Popular in thumri and dadra genres.

name: Khamaj
pakad: G M P D n D P M G R S
description: A light and romantic raga used in thumri, dadra, and folk music. The shuddha Ni in arohana and komal Ni in avarohana is its defining feature. Many well-known film songs are based on Khamaj.

name: Bilawal
pakad: G M D P M G R S
description: Uses all shuddha (natural) swaras, making it the closest Hindustani equivalent of the Western major scale. Associated with morning freshness and clarity. Despite its simplicity, highly nuanced in performance.

name: Darbari Kanada
pakad: S R g M P d n d P M g R S
description: Said to have been created by the legendary Miyan Tansen for the court of Emperor Akbar. A raga of immense dignity and gravitas, performed deep in the night. The andolan (oscillation) on komal Ga and komal Dha is its defining ornament.

name: Bageshri
pakad: g M D n S' n D M g R S
description: A raga of deep emotion and longing, typically associated with a lover awaiting reunion. Pa is avoided in the ascent. One of the most beloved ragas in thumri and khyal alike.

name: Kedar
pakad: M M M(t) P / N D P / M M R S
description: Unique in its use of both shuddha and tivra Ma. Re and Ga are avoided in the ascent. The repetition of Ma in the pakad is characteristic. A dignified raga used widely in bhajans and dhrupad.

name: Malkaus
pakad: S g M d n S'
description: A pentatonic raga (audav-audav) — Re and Pa are completely absent. Performed only in the dead of night, it has a profoundly introspective and mysterious character. Associated with deep meditation and one of the most atmospheric ragas in the repertoire.

name: Charukeshi
pakad: S R G M P d n S'
description: A raga that uniquely combines shuddha Re and Ga with komal Dha and Ni, creating a bittersweet character. Closely related to the Carnatic raga of the same name; its combination of notes does not fit neatly into any of the ten Hindustani thaats. Popular in Hindustani vocal and instrumental music.

name: Hameer
pakad: G M M(t) P D / N D P M G R S
description: A raga of strength and romance performed late in the evening. Like Kedar, Hameer uses both shuddha and tivra Ma, but includes Re and Ga in the ascent, giving it a fuller, more assertive character. The descent through tivra Ma then shuddha Ma is its most distinctive feature.

name: Sindhi Bhairavi
pakad: S r g M P / d n D P M g r S
description: The semi-classical variant of Bhairavi, distinguished by its use of shuddha Dha (and occasionally shuddha Ni) as ornamental touches against the otherwise komal scale. This freedom gives Sindhi Bhairavi a warmer, more folk-inflected quality than pure Bhairavi and makes it the predominant form heard in thumri and semi-classical performance.

name: Desh
pakad: N S R / M P N S' / n D P M G R S
description: A popular raga of the monsoon season, evoking the joy of rain and youthful romance. Its pentatonic ascent (omitting Ga and Dha) gives it a buoyant, folk-like quality, while the full descent through all seven swaras adds richness. Widely used in thumri, dadra, and film music.

name: Kaushik Dhwani
pakad: S g M d n S'
description: A pentatonic raga that omits both Re and Pa entirely, using the same five swaras as Malkaus — Sa, komal Ga, Ma, komal Dha, and komal Ni. Traditionally treated as a separate raga in some lineages, the distinction from Malkaus lies in subtle differences of phrasing and emphasis rather than scale. Creates a similarly introspective atmosphere, suited to deep concentration in the late hours of the night.

name: Shobhavari
pakad: r M P d S' / d P M r S
description: A dawn raga from Bhairav thaat. Ga is entirely absent, giving it a sparse, open quality distinct from Bhairav itself. The pentatonic ascent (omitting Ga and Ni) and the prominent komal Re create a serene, devotional atmosphere well suited to early morning.

name: Shree
pakad: r M M(t) P d N S'
description: One of the most austere and spiritually weighty ragas. Ga is entirely absent. The vadi komal Re combined with both shuddha and tivra Ma creates a very distinctive color. Associated with winter evenings and devotional singing.

name: Bhimpalasi
pakad: komal Ga Ma Pa / komal Ni Dha Pa Ma komal Ga / Re Sa
description: An audav-shadav raga (five notes ascending, six descending) that omits Re and Ni in the ascent, giving it a sense of yearning and emotional restraint. The komal Ga and komal Ni against shuddha Ma create a distinctive wistfulness. One of the most beloved afternoon ragas, equally at home in dhrupad, khyal, and thumri.

name: Bihag
pakad: Ga tivra Ma Pa / Ni SA Re SA Ni / Dha Pa Ma Ga Re Sa
description: A popular night raga whose defining feature is the use of tivra Ma in vakra (zigzag) phrases during both ascent and descent, even though the base scale belongs to Bilawal thaat. Both shuddha and tivra Ma appear in the scale; the pentatonic ascent (omitting Re and Dha) gives it an open, lyrical quality. Widely used in khyal and film music, it carries a mood of quiet romance.

name: Hamsadhwani
pakad: Sa Re Ga Pa Ni SA
description: A pentatonic raga (audav-audav) that completely omits Ma and Dha, giving it a bright, unencumbered quality. Originally from Carnatic music (derived from the 29th melakarta Shankarabharanam), it was adopted into Hindustani music and is now a popular concert opener. Its buoyant, auspicious character makes it ideal for evening performance.

name: Bhupali
pakad: Ga Re Ga Pa Dha Pa Ga Re Sa
description: A pentatonic evening raga (audav-audav) using only Sa Re Ga Pa Dha, completely omitting Ma and Ni. Classified under Kalyan thaat as occasional use of tivra Ma as a passing ornament is permitted. Equivalent to the Carnatic Mohanam and closely related to the Western pentatonic major scale. Its open, spacious quality makes it one of the most universally appealing ragas.

name: Multani
pakad: komal Ga tivra Ma Pa / komal Ni Dha Pa tivra Ma komal Ga / komal Re Sa
description: A Todi thaat raga performed in the later afternoon, distinguished from Miyan ki Todi by its vadi (Pa rather than komal Dha) and its audav ascent that omits Re and Dha. The tivra Ma and komal Ga create the characteristic Todi tension, while the vadi Pa anchors it with greater stability. A raga of deep contemplation and quiet intensity.

name: Jaunpuri
pakad: Re Ma Pa / komal Ni komal Dha Pa Ma komal Ga Re Sa
description: A raga of Asavari thaat performed in the morning, distinguished by its audav ascent (omitting komal Ga and Ni) and its use of shuddha Dha in the ascent against komal Dha in the descent. This contrast gives Jaunpuri a subtle harmonic color shift between rise and fall. A raga of dignified sadness and devotional depth.

name: Chandrakauns
pakad: Sa komal Ga Ma / komal Dha Ni SA / Ni komal Dha Ma komal Ga Sa
description: A pentatonic late-night raga (audav-audav) that omits Re and Pa entirely. Distinguished from Malkaus by its use of shuddha Ni in place of komal Ni, lending it a subtly brighter, more luminous quality befitting its lunar name (Chandra = moon). The characteristic phrases emphasize movement around Ma and komal Dha. A raga for deep concentration and nocturnal contemplation.

name: Durga
pakad: Sa Re Ma Pa Dha SA / Dha Pa Ma Re Sa
description: A pentatonic night raga (audav-audav) that omits Ga and Ni entirely, using only the five shuddha swaras Sa Re Ma Pa Dha. Its scalar movement is identical to the ascent of Desh but the two ragas are distinguished by characteristic phrases, vadi-samvadi treatment, and emotional register — Durga is more majestic and self-contained. Named after the goddess Durga, it carries a sense of power and auspiciousness.

name: Madhuvanti
pakad: Re komal Ga tivra Ma Pa Ni SA / Dha Pa tivra Ma komal Ga Re Sa
description: A relatively modern raga conventionally grouped under Todi thaat on account of its komal Ga and tivra Ma, though its shuddha Re and shuddha Dha depart from the strict Todi scale. The name means 'sweet like honey' and it carries a more approachable, lyrical quality compared to the austere gravity of Miyan ki Todi. The tivra Ma and komal Ga retain the characteristic Todi tension, but the fuller scale and afternoon timing create a warmer, more intimate atmosphere.

name: Pilu
pakad: komal Ga Ma Pa / komal Ni Dha Pa Ma komal Ga Re Sa
description: One of the most liberal ragas in Hindustani music — in practice, both komal and shuddha Ga and both komal and shuddha Ni (and occasionally komal Re) appear, making Pilu a raga of deliberate expressive ambiguity. Primarily a semi-classical and thumri-dadra raga with no prescribed time of performance. Its folk-derived quality and playful character make it popular in light classical and film music.
